chunk_summary: Refeeding syndrome in SAM requires early recognition and management with electrolyte correction, thiamine supplementation, and cautious feeding advancement to prevent clinical deterioration.
learning_objectives: Recognize clinical signs and risk factors of refeeding syndrome in severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Understand the role of electrolyte monitoring and replacement in managing refeeding syndrome.
Learn appropriate feeding strategies to prevent and manage refeeding syndrome.
Identify the importance of thiamine supplementation and infection management in SAM.
key_facts: Refeeding syndrome risk is high in SAM due to depleted potassium, magnesium, and phosphate stores.
Clinical signs include tachypnea, hypoxia, arrhythmias, neuromuscular weakness, seizures, or worsening edema within 2–5 days of feeding advancement.
Routine supplementation: potassium (3–4 mmol/kg/day) and magnesium (0.4–0.6 mmol/kg/day) even without lab confirmation.
Thiamine should be added before further caloric escalation where feasible.
Moderate-to-severe hypophosphatemia requires phosphate replacement, with IV phosphate indicated for severe cases under cardiac and calcium monitoring.
Feeding advancement should be slowed if phosphate products or testing are unavailable, with urgent referral arranged if necessary.
reference_quote: Refeeding syndrome risk is predictable in SAM because potassium, magnesium, and phosphate stores are depleted and carbohydrate-driven insulin release after feeding shifts these electrolytes intracellularly. (Source: WHO, Pocket book of hospital care for children, NBK154454)
